coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence origin and chirality mechanism of graphene quantum Dots: Twist or Non-Twist?
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Lichun
Sun, Mengtao
Zheng, Youjin
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, we theoretically investigate the fluorescence origin and chirality mechanism of graphene quantum dots with non-twist and twist geometries, respectively. It is revealed that twist is not necessary for fluorescence; but twist is must for the chirality, which can significantly enhance the intensity of chirality, demonstrated by ECD spectra. Our results provide deeper understanding on the physical mechanism of fluorescence and chirality of graphene quantum dot influenced by geometric twist.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence Origin
Chirality Mechanism
Graphene Quantum Dots
Twist or Non- Twist

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Science Foundation of China ( 11874407 , 91436102 and 11374353 ) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities in China ( 06500067 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optimized ground-state geometries of (a) DHQP (5,14-dihydroquinoxalino[2,3-b] phenazine), (b) G3 (GQD with one side of three atoms), (c) G5 (GQD with one side of five atoms), (d) G3-DHQP1 (the edge of G3 connected to one DHQP), (e) G3-DHQP2 (the edge of G5 connected to two DHQPs), (f) G5-DHQP (three DHQPs embedded in G5), (g) FG3-DHQP1 (the edge of G3-DHQP1 connected to functional groups) and (h) FG5-DHQP (the edge of G5-DHQP connected to functional groups).
ESP surface extrema of (a) DHQP, (b) G3, (c) G5, (d) G3-DHQP1, (e) G3-DHQP2, (f) G5-DHQP, (g) FG3-DHQP1 and (h)FG5-DHQP.

Absorption spectra, fluorescence spectra, and maps of CDD to them, where the green and red represent holes and electrons, respectively. (a) Absorption spectra and CDD of DHQP. (b) Fluorescence spectra and CDD of DHQP. (c) Absorption spectra and CDD of G3. (d) Absorption spectra and CDD of G5.

Characterizations of G3-DHQP2. (a) Absorption spectra and CDD of G3-DHQP2. (b) Fluorescence spectra and CDD of the first excited state of G3-DHQP2. (c) Fluorescence spectra and CDD of the second excited state of G3-DHQP2. (d) The DOS of the G3-DHQP2. (e) The UV–vis of the G3-DHQP2.

Characterizations of G3-DHQP1 (a) Absorption spectra and CDD of G3-DHQP1 (b) Absorption spectra of G3-DHQP1 and G3-DHQP2. (c) Fluorescence spectra and CDD of the third excited state of G3-DHQP1 (d) The DOS of the G3-DHQP1. (e) The UV–vis of the G3-DHQP1.
Absorption spectra of G5-DHQP and CDD in different excited states. (a) at the range of 300–900 nm, (b) 900–3000 nm.
(a) Absorption spectrum and CDD in specific interval of DHQP, G3, G3-DHQP2. (b) Absorption spectrum of G5 and G5-DHQP.
ECD spectroscopy of different condition (a) G3-DHQP1, (b) G3-DHQP2, (c) G3-DHQP1 compare with G3-DHQP2, (d) G5-DHQP, (e) FG3-DHQP1, and (f) G3-DHQP1compare with FG3-DHQP1.
Absorption spectra of FG3-DHQP1 and G3-DHQP1 compare with F-1DHQP. (a) the absorption spectra of FG3-DHQP1, (b) the absorption spectra of G3-DHQP1 and FG3-DHQP1.
Maps of CDD for the first five excited states (a-e) of FG3-DHQP1.
The side view and the absorption spectra of FG5-DHQP. (a) the side view of FG5-DHQP, (b) the absorption spectra of FG5-DHQP, (c) the absorption spectra of G5-DHQP and FG5-DHQP.
The CDD maps of FG5-DHQP (the excited states at (a)S1, (b)S4, (c)S6, (d)S12, (e)S24, (f)S26, (g)S41 and (f)S62).
ECD spectroscopy of FG5-DHQP and compared with G5-DHQP.
Size and solvent dependent absorption spectra. (a) solvent effect, (b) size dependent in ethanol solvent.
Charge transfer length of DHQP, G3, G5, G3-DHQP1, G3-DHQP2, where the unit is Å.
Charge transfer length of G5-DHQP, where the unit is Å.

head.ce:title: Fluorescence origin and chirality mechanism of graphene quantum Dots: Twist or Non-Twist?

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics and Electronic Engineering, Mudanjiang Normal University, Mudanjiang 157011, PR. China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physics and Electronic Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physics and Electronic Engineering, Mudanjiang Normal University, Mudanjiang 157011, PR. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mathematics and Physics, University of Science and Technology Beijing, Beijing 100083, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Science and Technology Beijing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Mathematics and Physics, University of Science and Technology Beijing, Beijing 100083, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, we theoretically investigate the fluorescence origin and chirality mechanism of graphene quantum dots with non-twist and twist geometries, respectively. It is revealed that twist is not necessary for fluorescence; but twist is must for the chirality, which can significantly enhance the intensity of chirality, demonstrated by ECD spectra. Our results provide deeper understanding on the physical mechanism of fluorescence and chirality of graphene quantum dot influenced by geometric twist.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as a novel sort of zero-dimensional (0D) carbon-based nanomaterials within 10 nm of ultra-fine size, has outstanding potential applications in many fields like biosensors, photocatalysis, organic solar cells and so on, because of their structural and functional features. [1–10] The fluorescence characteristic of GQDs is its most striking feature. The photoluminescence (PL) mechanism of the GQDs is determined by dissimilar textural types, their luminous properties are quite amusing. Some GQDs so far as to exhibit Up-Conversion Photoluminescence with emission wavelengths longer than other excitation wavelengths, compared to traditional PL mechanisms. The PL performance of GQDs is mainly influenced by its intrinsic condition of the central structure. [11–14] Besides, the PL mechanism of GQDs is also primarily affected by the edge state of the surface and π-conjugated domain, and the twisted surface is considered to be the dominating condition for producing fluorescence. [14,15].

Graphene is a two-dimensional (2D) layer of carbon atoms with sp [2] hybridization and honeycomb lattice permutation. With their unique atomic thickness and construction, GQDs are provided with excellent photoelectric characteristics count on their graphene lattices, which can be regarded as a single layer or several layers of the crystal structure of the graphene. [16–18] In recent years, the researches on the combination of chirality and GQDs came out, and the first chirality nanoparticles were synthesized by the method which is similar to the pattern of GQDs in 2016. [19–22] Synthetic CQDs usually contain carbon (C), hydrogen (H) and oxygen (O), single-layer graphene sheets can be used to cover the edge of graphene by chiral molecules to form a surface functionalization to make GQDs attain a twisted condition. [22] For bilayer graphene materials, twisted GQDs will be chiral by rotating two sheets of graphene relative to each other. [23–26] The GQDs are provided with chirality and the symmetry changed from high to low, at the time of the geometry of the GQDs is distorted by the surface functionalization of GQDs. The red shifts in the absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectrum could be observed. Chirality results in that holes reduce to localize at the edge of the GQDs, and electrons can simply be transferred to other parts because of the reduced symmetry of CQDs resulting in electron delocalization. Thus, the consequence is that under the action of many charges transfer and favorable-separated electron-hole (e-h) pairs in condition, the ability of GQDs to bring out fluorescence is significantly increased. [27].

Therefore, it is not uncommon to use GQDs as chiral fluorescent materials. [15,28] It is well known that the fluorescence and twisted planar structure of GQDs are inextricably linked, and the chirality of GQDs is influenced by the structure of chiral crystal at the edge [29]. However, if graphene does not twist by surface functionalization, is it still possible to produce fluorescence? If possible, it will be an important breakthrough. The fluorescence generated by “twist” is always uncertain. And the fluorescence intensity and wavelength will be seriously affected by “twist”, which has a prodigious impact on the fluorescence effect. [30] Besides, the recent research has new synthesis methods of GQDs, which are planar. While, researches on GQDs in non-Twist state is emerging. The optical properties and fluorescence sources in non-twisted GQDs, as well as its comparison with twisted state could be further studied.

In this paper, we will theoretically explain the physical mechanism of GQDs that can produce fluorescence without twist. By comparing the charge delocalization and other optical properties of fluorescent molecules with GQDs containing fluorescent molecules, the theoretical results obtained not only reveal the origin of fluorescence, but also reveal its regulation. Our results deepen our understanding of the fluorescence mechanism of GQDs in the case of “no-twist”. At the same time, our result explained the principle of GQDs producing fluorescence under plane conditions, and its future application in optics will be revealed here.

Molecular structures can be seen in Fig. 1 . The whole quantum calculations were performed with Gaussian 16 software. [31] Molecular geometries were optimized with density functional theory (DFT), [32] B3LPY functional, [33] and 6–31 g(d) basis set for the ground state. Their optical absorptions were calculated by means of using time-dependent DFT (TD-DFT), [34] the CAM-B3LYP functional, [35] and the 6–31 g(d) basis set. Their geometries of the GQDs at the excited state were optimized with TD-DFT, [34] the B3LYP functional, and the 6–31 g(d) basis set. Based on the optimized excited geometries, the fluorescence of GQDs was obtained with TD-DFT, the CAM-B3LYP functional, and the 6–31 g(d) basis set. The electron excitation analysis was performed by the Multiwfn 3.6 program. [36] The maps of charge difference density (CDD) were drawn by using VMD software. [37].

The evaluation of molecular electrostatic potential (ESP) is a performance bottleneck for many computational chemical tasks like restrained ESP charge fitting or quantum mechanics/molecular mechanics simulations. [38] Fig. 2 is the electrostatic potential (ESP) colored molecular surface map, and in which blue, white and red correspond to ESP varying from minimum to maximum kcal/mol, the green and orange spheres correspond to the minima and maxima of ESP surface, respectively. The larger the alteration of the positive and negative are, the more uneven the charge distribution. Fig. 2(a), (b) and (c) are the ESP diagrams for fluorophore (DHQP) and graphene of two sizes, respectively. It can be found that the value in Fig. 2(a) (DHQP) is relatively large and the charge distribution isn’t an equation. In Fig. 2(b) and (c), the value of pure graphene is small and the charge transfer is relatively uniform. While, compared with pure graphene, the value of graphene with DHQP (d)(e)(f) increases, which indicates that the ground state charge distribution is already uneven. This promotes the delocalization of excited state charge and leads to the increase of charge-hole recombination condition in the process of fluorescence, which makes the fluorescence intensity actually intense.

Fig. 3 (a) reveals the absorption spectra of the fluorophore in detail, the characteristic of the DHQP for charge transfer is investigated simultaneously, where the green and red represent holes and electrons, severally. It is found that there are three strong peaks in these absorption spectra of DHQP. In these strong absorptions, the low-energy excited state of DHQP is around 400 nm, at this point, electrons being shifting from the nitrogen (N) side connect with hydrogen (H) to the N side only. The other two strong peaks are in the 225 nm to 250 nm range, and the electrons and holes are evenly staggered distributed for the state with the strongest absorption peaks, the other one's electrons are concentrated in the molecule around N, which is not attached to H. Fluorescence spectra and charge transfer maps of DHQP are shown in Fig. 3(b), the fluorescence peak is close to 425 nm, the electron and hole recombine, electron-hole distribution in fluorescence is shown obviously. Owing to the comparatively obvious charge transfer, DHQP has fluorescent properties. Fig. 3(c) and Fig. 3(d) show the absorption spectra of graphene at two different sizes respectively, and the charge transfer diagrams at their first three excited states. By comparison, diverse sizes of graphene have identical distribution of electrons and holes in the same size range and same excited states, which suggests that they share the same excited state property. While, as the size of graphene increases, there is a redshift in the absorption spectrum, which is attributed to the heightened π-electron delocalization with increasing conjugation length, resulting in a reduced electron–phonon coupling strength. [39] And due to the enhancement of the localized excitation, the absorption strength will increase. In charge transfer diagrams, charge transfer and local excitation cannot be determined only by the positions of electrons and holes. From Tables 1 and 2
, it can determine that the length of charge transfer occurs in the molecule, to determine whether it's charge transfer or local excitation. The value greater than 2 indicates charge transfer, and the value less than 2 indicates local excitation. In addition, the degree of charge transfer determines the absorption intensity and fluorescence intensity. The greater the charge transfer capacity, the greater the fluorescence capacity. In the table, the charge transfer length of G3 and G5 is basically 0, so it must be local excitation, and the rest parts occur charge transfer.

The charge transfer corresponding to the absorption spectra of different excited states are expressed in Fig. 4 (a). The fluorescence spectrum and its charge transfer diagram are shown in Fig. 4(b) and 4(c). To analyze charge transfer of G3-DHQP2 in the absorption spectrum, we redistribute the molecules, defining graphene as the frag 1 and the two DHQPs as the frag 2. It is not difficult to find there are not only the charge transfer between 1 and frag 2, but also the transfer between graphene and DHQP combined with Fig. 4(d) (DOS) in Fig. 3(a). In order to explore the direction of charge transfer in more details, the UV–vis (Fig. 4(e)) diagram is made. Fig. 4(a), 4(d) and 4(e) are integrated to analyze the charge transfer under these four excited states. Firstly, by observing the charge-transfer diagrams of S1 and S2, it can be intuitively perceived that the distribution of the charges and holes on frag 1 is relatively uniform. Observing Fig. 4(e), the charge transfer of frag 1 itself is obvious, while the others are comparatively weak, which can explain the phenomenon of charge transfer diagram. Then, exploring S3 and S4, in the charge-transfer diagram here, the charge and the hole are mostly distributed separately in these two parts can be seen. Through UV–vis (Fig. 4(e)), we can see that the charge transfer between frag 1 and frag 2 is significantly stronger than the charge transfer between the two parts themselves. In summary, S1 and S2 are mostly self-charge transfer of graphene, and charge transfer between fragments is more prominent for S3 and S4. There is weak fluorescence from S1 and S2 in Fig. 4(b) and 4(c), where charge transfer is shown in the insert. Thus, the fluorescence should come from higher excited states. we try to optimize the geometry of S3 excited state, but is not successful.

To investigate the source of fluorescence, a patterned molecule is formed by a single DHQP attached to the edge of graphite. Fig. 5 (a) shows its charge-transfer and absorption spectra. At about 400 nm, there is a strong absorption peak of the third excited state, and the charge transfer is shown here. It is found that the charge transfer of the part of DHQP is greatly similar to that of the individual DHQP molecules at about 400 nm. Fig. 5(b) shows the absorption spectra of two forming molecules linked to one DHQP (G-DHQP1) and two DHQPS (G-DHQP2). In each of them, there is a strong peak at 400 nm, and G-DHQP2 is about twice as high as G-DHQP1. Doubling the number of DHQPs also doubles the height of the strong peak, due to the number of DHQP molecules attached to it. Fig. (c) shows the fluorescence spectra and charge transfer of the third excited state of G-DHQP1. The strong peak of the third excited state is at 400 nm, and the charge transfer diagram is shown in the Fig. It is found that the charge transfer of the connected DHQP part on G-DHQP1 is basically the same as that of the single DHQP. Fig. 5(d) and Fig. 5(e) are the DOS and UV–vis of G-DHQP1, respectively. We define graphene as frag 1 and DHQP as frag 2. It is found that around 400 nm, which is the third excited state, the charge transfer of frag 2 of DHQP is very obvious. In other words, the strong absorption is mainly contributed by DHQP at this time. Therefore, in summary, we can conclude that DHQPs at the edge link has a significant influence on the formed molecular fluorescence properties, and the corresponding optical properties are the contribution of DHQPs.

Three models of DHQP molecules embedded in graphene were designed. The absorption spectra and charge-transfer diagrams of G5-DHQP were made to analysis. Fig. 6 (a) shows the absorption spectrum and its charge transfer at 400 nm to 900 nm wavelength, and Fig. 6(b) shows 900 nm to 3000 nm. Through these two pictures, it can be found that there are numerous strong absorption peaks in the wavelength range, and their relative charge transfer graphs have obvious charge transfer correspondingly. Compared with normal graphene, DHQP embedded into the graphene molecules produced a substantial increase in charge transfer length. This shows that DHQP affects graphene, making molecules embedded in DHQP to have fluorescence characteristics.

Fig. 7 (a) shows the absorption spectra and charge transfer maps of G3, G3-DHQP2 and DHQP. In Fig 0.7(a), we can effortlessly discover the position of two strong peaks of G3-DHQP2 is strongly similar to G3 and DHQP. But, by looking at the fluorescence spectra of G5 and G5-DHQP in Fig. 7(b), it can be observed, there is a no strong peak of G5 in the strong peak range of G5-DHQP. Combining what is shown in the above two figures, we can draw a conclusion, that the influence of DHQP on G3-DHQP2 or G5-DHQP is much greater than that of graphene.

It has reported that chirality of GQDs is powerfully dependent on the twist geometries of GQDs. Consequently, the above molecular models are surface-edge functionalized. To reveal the relationship between chirality and twist of GQDs, the ECD spectra of non-twist geometries, seeing Fig. 8 (a)-(d), and twist geometry, see Fig. 8(e)-(f). It is found that for the non-twist geometries, the intensities of ECD are quite feeble, seeing Fig. 8(a)-(d), although the increase in structural asymmetry in Fig. 8(b) increases the strength of ECD slightly more than the increase in structural asymmetry in Fig. 8(a), seeing comparison in Fig. 8(c). For the twisted geometry (see insert in Fig. 8(e)), the ECD spectrum of FG3-DHQP1 in Fig. 8(e) is significantly enhanced up to 105 times, by comparing Fig. 8(a) and 8(e), where the direct comparison can be seen from Fig. 8(f). Thus, the twist can strongly enhance the chirality, demonstrated by ECD.

The absorption spectrum of FG3-DHQP1 is shown in Fig. 9 (a). The evident red shifted can be observed in Fig. 9 (b), which is the absorption spectrum of the G3-DHQP1 and FG3-DHQP1. Compared with G3-DHQP1, the red shifted can ascribe to the production of twist geometries of FG3-DHQP1, because the abundant surface functional groups containing CO/CN bonds are added to the edge of the surface.

Fig. 10 reveals the influence of edge functionalization and the twist of FG3-DHQP1 for the charge transfer. The influence of charge transfer caused by functional groups at the edge of FG3-DHQP1 is extremely little. But the electrons and holes of FG3-DHQP1 are obviously delocalized. And the electrons and hole are asymmetrically distributed, due to the twist of geometries of FG3-DHQP1. The main reason for the large delocalization of FG3-DHQP1 is the broken-down of centrosymmetry and inversion symmetry of structural twist caused by edge functionalization.

In order to further confirm our idea, we also performed surface functionalization of G5-DHQP. The pictures show the side view and the absorption spectra of G5-DHQP in Fig. 11 (a). By observing the picture of FG5-DHQP, it can be found that the G5-DHQP with the surface-edge functionalization has obvious twisting. The absorption spectra of G5-DHQP as shown in Fig. 11(b), there are several strong peaks in the spectra. The absorption peaks are relatively dense from 350 nm to 1300 nm, and the absorption peaks are also strong but sparse at the range of 1300 nm − 5000 nm. Comparing G5-DHQP with FG5-DHQP, a significant red-shift appears as shown in Fig. 11(c).

Several strongly excited states are shown in the absorption spectrum shown in the figure above, so the CDD maps of these peaks are made in Fig. 12 . In these images, they all have a remarkable feature, which is relatively obvious charge transfer. The reason for this condition is the chirality of the twist.

In like manner, the ECD spectroscopy of G5-DHQP was made (shown in Fig. 13 (a)). Then it can be found in Fig. 13(b), the intensity of G5-DHQP is almost negligible compared to FG5-DHQP, because the multiples of intensity between them are so large. As a result, like the FG3-DHQP, the phenomenon shown by G5-DHQP further confirms that twist can increase the chirality of GQDs.

Fig. 14 reveals that calculated results are strongly dependent on solvent and size. in our manuscript, we focused on the physical mechanism, not filigree comparison with experimental results. Qualitatively, the absorption spectra will be red shifted when solvent is considered, see Fig. 14(a) as well as red shift with the increase of sizes in solvent, see Fig. 14(b).

The fluorescence physical mechanism of GQDs, which are combined with DHQP in different methods and whether chiral or not is explained theoretically. The results show that both GQDs combined with DHQP and GQDs experienced surface functionalization under the same conditions and have strong charge transfer and fluorescence characteristics. This means that GQDs can possess fluorescence even with no-twist and extraordinary tiny chirality. Our results not only deepen our new understanding of planar GQDs, but also provide insights into their future applications on the nanoscale.

Lichun Sun: Data curation, Visualization, Investigation, Formal analysis. Mengtao Sun: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Youjin Zheng: Conceptualization, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Science Foundation of China (11874407, 91436102 and 11374353) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities in China (06500067).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly fluorescent carbon dots as selective and visual probes for sensing copper ions in living cells via an electron transfer process

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Rapid screening and quantitative detection of Salmonella using a quantum dot nanobeadbased biosensor

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene-based SERS for sensor and catalysis

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Magnetic Networks of Carbon Quantum Dots and Ag Particles

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile green and one-pot synthesis of seville orange derived carbon dots as a fluorescent sensor for Fe3+ Ions

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of modified carbon dots with performance of ultraviolet absorption used in sunscreen

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Kim, S.; Hwang, S.W.; Kim, M.-K.; Shin, D.Y.; Shin, D.H.; Kim, C.O.; Yang, S.B.; Park, J.H.; Hwang, E.; Choi, S.-H.; et al. Anomalous behaviors of visible luminescence from graphene quantum dots: Interplay between size and shape. ACS Nano 2012, 6, 8203–8208.

Investigation of photoluminescence mechanism of graphene quantum dots and evaluation of their assembly into polymer dots. Carbon 2014, 77, 462–472.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Influence of group modification at the edges of carbon quantum dots on fluorescent emission

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Tang, J.; Zhang, J.; Zhang, Y.F.; Xiao, Y.M.; Chen, Y.H.; Ding, L. Influence of Group Modification at the Edges of Carbon Quantum Dots on Fluorescent Emission. Nanoscale Res. Lett. 2019, 14, 241

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Roll-to-roll production of 30-inch graphene films for transparent electrodes
